name: Introduction to Psychology
description: An exploration of human behavior and mental processes — from neurons and perception to personality, social influence, and therapy — covering the major perspectives and key findings of modern psychology.

modules.title: What Is Psychology?
modules.objectives: Define psychology; identify major theoretical perspectives; understand the history from Wundt to modern approaches; recognize psychology as a science.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 1: Introduction to Psychology
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Psychology #1 — Intro to Psychology
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — History & Approaches (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Choose two psychological perspectives (e.g., behavioral and cognitive). Write a short paragraph explaining how each would interpret the same behavior — for example, why a student procrastinates. Compare the explanations.

modules.title: Research Methods in Psychology
modules.objectives: Distinguish experimental, correlational, and descriptive research; identify variables; understand ethics in psychological research.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Research Methods in Psychology
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Research Methods (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Design a simple experiment to test the hypothesis that background music affects memory recall. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, control group, and one potential confound. Explain how you would obtain informed consent.

modules.title: Biological Bases of Behavior
modules.objectives: Describe neuron structure and function; identify key neurotransmitters and their effects; locate major brain structures and their roles.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — The Neuron & Neural Firing
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Biological Bases (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Research one neurotransmitter (e.g., dopamine, serotonin, GABA). Write a one-page summary explaining its function, which behaviors or disorders it is associated with, and one drug or therapy that targets it.

modules.title: Sensation and Perception
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensation from perception; explain absolute and difference thresholds; describe Gestalt principles and depth cues.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 5: Sensation and Perception
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Vision and the Eye
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Sensation & Perception (practice)
modules.assignment: Find three optical illusions online. For each, explain which Gestalt principle or perceptual concept (e.g., figure-ground, closure, depth cue) is involved and why our brain is "fooled."

modules.title: States of Consciousness
modules.objectives: Describe sleep stages and their functions; explain theories of dreaming; understand how hypnosis and psychoactive drugs alter consciousness.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 4: States of Consciousness
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — States of Consciousness (practice)
modules.assignment: Keep a sleep journal for 3 days. Record your bedtime, wake time, any dreams you recall, and how rested you feel (1–10). Analyze your data in relation to what you learned about sleep stages and circadian rhythms.

modules.title: Learning and Conditioning
modules.objectives: Explain classical and operant conditioning with examples; describe schedules of reinforcement; apply observational learning theory.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Learning (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Identify one example of classical conditioning and one example of operant conditioning from your own life or from a TV show/movie. Label all relevant components (US, UR, CS, CR for classical; reinforcer/punisher type for operant).

modules.title: Memory
modules.objectives: Describe encoding, storage, and retrieval; compare short-term and long-term memory; explain forgetting and memory distortion.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Memory (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Test the serial position effect on yourself: memorize a list of 12 unrelated words, wait 2 minutes, then write down as many as you recall. Circle which positions (beginning, middle, end) you remembered best. Explain your results using the primacy and recency effects.

modules.title: Thinking and Intelligence
modules.objectives: Describe problem-solving strategies and cognitive biases; compare theories of intelligence; discuss the nature–nurture debate in intelligence.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 7: Thinking and Intelligence
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Problem Solving and Decision Making
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Cognition (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Describe a real-life decision you made recently. Identify at least two cognitive biases (e.g., confirmation bias, availability heuristic, anchoring) that may have influenced your thinking. How could you have decided more rationally?

modules.title: Developmental Psychology
modules.objectives: Describe Piaget's and Erikson's stage theories; explain attachment theory; apply developmental concepts to adolescence and adulthood.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Piaget's Stages of Cognitive Development
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Developmental Psychology (practice)
modules.assignment: Interview a family member or friend from a different generation (a young child, a teenager, or an older adult). Write a one-page reflection connecting what you observed to a concept from Piaget's or Erikson's stage theory.

modules.title: Motivation and Emotion
modules.objectives: Apply Maslow's hierarchy of needs; compare theories of emotion; distinguish intrinsic from extrinsic motivation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 10: Emotion and Motivation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Motivation & Emotion (practice)
modules.assignment: Apply Maslow's hierarchy to a character from a book, film, or TV show. Identify which level of the hierarchy that character is primarily operating from and provide specific evidence from the story. Suggest what they would need to move to the next level.

modules.title: Personality
modules.objectives: Compare major personality theories (psychoanalytic, humanistic, trait); describe the Big Five; distinguish projective from objective personality tests.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Personality (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Research one of the Big Five personality traits. Describe how psychologists measure it, what outcomes it predicts (career, relationships, health), and reflect on where you think you would score on that trait and why.

modules.title: Social Psychology
modules.objectives: Explain attribution theory; describe conformity, obedience, and group dynamics; apply social psychological principles to real-world behavior.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Social Psychology (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Describe a time when you conformed to group pressure or witnessed someone else conforming. Analyze the situation using Asch's conformity research or Milgram's obedience research. What factors made conformity more or less likely?

modules.title: Psychological Disorders
modules.objectives: Describe the DSM-5 classification system; identify symptoms of major anxiety, mood, and psychotic disorders; apply the diathesis-stress model.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Psychological Disorders
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Psychological Disorders (practice)
modules.assignment: Choose one psychological disorder (not one affecting you personally) and write a one-page research summary covering: main diagnostic criteria, estimated prevalence, one biological factor, and one environmental factor that contribute to it.

modules.title: Treatment and Therapy
modules.objectives: Compare types of psychotherapy; explain how CBT works; describe common medications used in mental health treatment; evaluate therapy effectiveness.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Chapter 16: Therapy and Treatment
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Types of Psychological Therapies
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Treatment (practice questions)
modules.assignment: Compare cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) to psychoanalysis. For each: describe the core technique used, the type of disorder it is best suited for, and one limitation. Which approach do you find more convincing based on the evidence?

questions.question: Which psychological perspective would most likely explain aggression in terms of unconscious childhood conflicts?
questions.explanation: The psychoanalytic perspective, founded by Freud, emphasizes unconscious drives and early childhood experiences as drivers of behavior.

questions.question: A researcher randomly assigns participants to treatment or control groups. This primarily controls for:
questions.explanation: Random assignment ensures that any pre-existing differences between participants are distributed equally across groups.

questions.question: Damage to the hippocampus would most directly impair:
questions.explanation: The hippocampus is critical for consolidating new declarative (explicit) memories into long-term storage.

questions.question: The Gestalt principle that states we tend to group nearby objects together is called:
questions.explanation: The proximity principle holds that we perceptually group objects that are physically close together.

questions.question: Research suggests that __________ sleep plays an important role in consolidating emotional memories and procedural skills.
questions.explanation: REM sleep is associated with the consolidation of emotional memories and procedural learning.

questions.question: A child is rewarded with candy every 5 math problems completed. This is a:
questions.explanation: A fixed-ratio schedule delivers reinforcement after a set number of responses (every 5 problems).

questions.question: The part of a neuron that carries signals AWAY from the cell body is the:
questions.explanation: Axons conduct electrical impulses away from the neuron's cell body toward the terminal buttons.

questions.question: According to the absolute threshold, a stimulus must be detectable __________ of the time.
questions.explanation: The absolute threshold is defined as the minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

questions.question: When a previously conditioned response disappears because the CS is repeatedly presented without the UCS, this is called:
questions.explanation: Extinction occurs when the conditioned stimulus no longer reliably predicts the unconditioned stimulus, and the conditioned response fades.

questions.question: The "fight-or-flight" stress response is controlled primarily by the:
questions.explanation: The sympathetic division of the autonomic nervous system triggers the fight-or-flight response.

questions.question: Which research method is best suited to establishing a cause-and-effect relationship?
questions.explanation: Only a controlled experiment — with manipulation of an independent variable and random assignment — can establish causation.

questions.question: George Miller found that short-term memory holds approximately __________ chunks of information.
questions.explanation: Miller's famous "magical number seven" paper described short-term memory capacity as 7 ± 2 chunks.

questions.question: Elizabeth Loftus's misinformation effect research demonstrated that:
options: Memory is a perfect recording of events
Only emotional events are reliably remembered
questions.explanation: Loftus showed that leading questions and post-event information can create false memories or distort existing ones.

questions.question: Which schedule of reinforcement produces the highest and most consistent rate of responding?
questions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules produce the highest, most persistent response rates because reinforcement is unpredictable — as in slot machines.

questions.question: Which perspective emphasizes the role of conscious thought processes in shaping behavior?
questions.explanation: The cognitive perspective focuses on mental processes such as thinking, reasoning, and problem-solving as the primary influences on behavior.

questions.question: Psychology is best defined as:
options: The study of the brain's physical structure
The scientific study of behavior and mental processes
The study of human society and culture
questions.answer: The scientific study of behavior and mental processes
questions.explanation: Psychology is the scientific discipline that studies both observable behavior and internal mental processes.

questions.question: In a controlled experiment, participants who do NOT receive the treatment belong to the:
questions.explanation: The control group does not receive the independent variable, providing a baseline against which to compare the experimental group.

questions.question: Which neurotransmitter deficit is most associated with Parkinson's disease?
questions.explanation: Parkinson's disease results from the degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra.

questions.question: The smallest detectable change in stimulus intensity is the:
questions.explanation: The JND (or difference threshold) is the minimum change in stimulation that can be detected 50% of the time.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as a stimulant?
questions.explanation: Caffeine is a stimulant that increases CNS activity by blocking adenosine receptors.

questions.question: A dog no longer salivates to a bell after the bell is repeatedly presented without food. This is:
questions.explanation: Extinction occurs when the conditioned stimulus is presented repeatedly without the unconditioned stimulus.

questions.question: Chunking improves short-term memory by:
questions.explanation: Chunking groups individual pieces of information into larger meaningful units, allowing more information to fit within memory limits.

questions.question: The ability to reason abstractly and solve novel problems is called:
questions.explanation: Fluid intelligence refers to the capacity for novel reasoning, independent of acquired knowledge.

questions.question: According to Piaget, understanding that the amount of liquid does not change when poured into a different container is called:
questions.explanation: Conservation is the understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in appearance, typically acquired in the concrete operational stage.

questions.question: According to Maslow, self-actualization represents:
questions.explanation: Self-actualization is the highest level of Maslow's hierarchy, representing the fulfillment of one's unique potential.

questions.question: The defense mechanism in which threatening thoughts are pushed into the unconscious is called:
questions.explanation: Repression is the unconscious blocking of anxiety-producing thoughts from conscious awareness.

questions.question: The bystander effect predicts that individuals are __________ likely to help in an emergency when many others are present.
questions.explanation: Diffusion of responsibility in larger groups reduces any individual's sense of personal obligation to act.

questions.question: A person experiencing at least two weeks of depressed mood, loss of interest, and changes in sleep/appetite likely has:
questions.explanation: DSM-5 requires at least two weeks of depressed mood plus at least 5 total symptoms for a major depressive episode diagnosis.

questions.question: Systematic desensitization is a behavioral technique most effective for:
questions.explanation: Systematic desensitization pairs relaxation with gradual exposure to feared stimuli to extinguish fear responses.

questions.question: Erik Erikson's stage of "Trust vs. Mistrust" occurs during:
questions.explanation: Erikson's first stage (0–18 months) centers on whether infants develop trust based on the reliability of caregivers.

questions.question: Which disorder is characterized by alternating episodes of mania and depression?
questions.explanation: Bipolar disorder involves cycles of extreme mood elevation (mania or hypomania) and depressive episodes.

questions.question: Watson and Rayner's "Little Albert" experiment demonstrated:
options: Classical conditioning of fear in humans
questions.answer: Classical conditioning of fear in humans
questions.explanation: Watson and Rayner classically conditioned fear of a white rat in Little Albert by pairing it with a loud noise.

questions.question: A therapist who helps a client identify and challenge distorted thinking patterns is using:
questions.explanation: CBT targets maladaptive thought patterns (cognitive distortions) and uses behavioral techniques to change them.

questions.question: SSRIs are most commonly prescribed for:
questions.explanation: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors increase serotonin availability and are first-line treatments for depression and many anxiety disorders.

questions.question: The biopsychosocial model proposes that mental disorders arise from:
options: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
questions.answer: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
questions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model holds that mental health and illness result from the interplay of genetic, psychological, and sociocultural influences.

quizQuestions.question: Psychology is best defined as:
options: The study of the brain's physical structure
The scientific study of behavior and mental processes
quizQuestions.answer: The scientific study of behavior and mental processes
quizQuestions.explanation: Psychology is the scientific discipline studying both observable behavior and internal mental processes.

quizQuestions.question: Wilhelm Wundt established the first psychology laboratory in:
quizQuestions.explanation: Wundt opened his lab in Leipzig, Germany in 1879, marking the formal beginning of experimental psychology.

quizQuestions.question: Which perspective focuses on unconscious drives and early childhood experiences?
quizQuestions.explanation: Freud's psychoanalytic perspective emphasizes unconscious conflicts and childhood experiences as drivers of behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The behavioral perspective was most strongly championed by:
quizQuestions.explanation: Skinner was the leading figure in behaviorism, emphasizing observable behavior and the role of reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: Using positive reinforcement to encourage a child's good behavior is an example of which psychological perspective in practice?
quizQuestions.explanation: Behavioral psychology focuses on how reinforcement and punishment shape observable actions.

quizQuestions.question: In an experiment, the variable the researcher manipulates is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The independent variable is deliberately changed to observe its effect on the dependent variable.

quizQuestions.question: Ice cream sales and drowning rates are positively correlated. The best explanation is:
quizQuestions.explanation: This is a classic example of a confounding variable (hot weather) creating a spurious correlation.

quizQuestions.question: The placebo effect is best controlled by using a:
quizQuestions.explanation: In a double-blind study, neither participants nor experimenters know who received the real treatment, controlling for expectation effects.

quizQuestions.question: Which ethical principle requires participants to be fully informed of risks before agreeing to participate?
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent requires researchers to explain risks and procedures before participants agree to join a study.

quizQuestions.question: Observing animals in their natural habitat without interfering is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Naturalistic observation records behavior in real-world settings without manipulation or interference.

quizQuestions.question: The gap between two neurons across which neurotransmitters travel is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The synapse is the tiny gap between neurons where chemical communication via neurotransmitters occurs.

quizQuestions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with pleasure and reward?
quizQuestions.explanation: Dopamine is central to the brain's reward pathway and is linked to feelings of pleasure and motivation.

quizQuestions.question: The brain structure most responsible for regulating breathing and heartbeat is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The medulla oblongata in the brainstem controls vital autonomic functions like breathing and heart rate.

quizQuestions.question: In most right-handed people, language is primarily processed in the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Language areas (Broca's and Wernicke's) are typically located in the left hemisphere in right-handed individuals.

quizQuestions.question: The limbic system is most associated with:
quizQuestions.explanation: The limbic system, including the amygdala and hippocampus, plays key roles in emotional responses and memory formation.

quizQuestions.question: The minimum intensity needed to detect a stimulus 50% of the time is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus level detectable half the time under ideal conditions.

quizQuestions.question: The Gestalt principle that we tend to "fill in" incomplete figures is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Closure describes the tendency to perceive incomplete shapes as complete whole figures.

quizQuestions.question: Gradually adjusting to low light after entering a dark room is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Dark adaptation is the process by which the eyes increase sensitivity to light in low-light conditions over several minutes.

quizQuestions.question: Which depth cue requires BOTH eyes to work together?
quizQuestions.explanation: Binocular disparity uses the slightly different images from each eye to judge depth — it is a binocular (two-eye) cue.

quizQuestions.question: The process converting physical stimuli into neural signals is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of physical energy (light, sound, etc.) into electrical neural signals that the brain can process.

quizQuestions.question: Which sleep stage is characterized by rapid eye movement and vivid dreaming?
quizQuestions.explanation: REM (rapid eye movement) sleep is when most vivid dreaming occurs and brain activity resembles that of wakefulness.

quizQuestions.question: According to Freud, the actual story and images of a dream represent the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The manifest content is the literal, surface-level storyline of the dream, as opposed to the latent (hidden symbolic) content.

quizQuestions.question: Hypnosis is best described as:
options: A state of heightened suggestibility and focused attention
A method of permanent mind control
quizQuestions.answer: A state of heightened suggestibility and focused attention
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypnosis is a trance-like state of focused attention and heightened openness to suggestion — not sleep or unconsciousness.

quizQuestions.question: A drug that reduces central nervous system activity is classified as a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Depressants (e.g., alcohol, benzodiazepines) slow CNS activity, reducing arousal and anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: The body's 24-hour biological clock is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The circadian rhythm is a roughly 24-hour internal cycle that regulates sleep, wakefulness, and other biological processes.

quizQuestions.question: In Pavlov's experiment, after conditioning the bell became a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Initially neutral, the bell became a conditioned stimulus after repeated pairing with food (the UCS).

quizQuestions.question: Positive reinforcement __________ a behavior; positive punishment __________ it.
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive reinforcement adds a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior; positive punishment adds an unpleasant stimulus to decrease it.

quizQuestions.question: A slot machine pays out on an unpredictable, random schedule. This is a __________ schedule of reinforcement.
quizQuestions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules deliver reinforcement after an unpredictable number of responses, producing high, steady response rates.

quizQuestions.question: Albert Bandura's Bobo doll experiments demonstrated:
quizQuestions.explanation: Children imitated aggressive behavior modeled by an adult, showing that learning can occur through observation alone.

quizQuestions.question: Taking aspirin to relieve a headache is an example of:
quizQuestions.explanation: Negative reinforcement removes an unpleasant stimulus (pain) to increase the likelihood of a behavior (taking aspirin).

quizQuestions.question: The three stages of memory processing in correct order are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Information must first be encoded, then stored, and finally retrieved when needed.

quizQuestions.question: George Miller found that short-term memory holds approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: Miller's "magical number seven" research established short-term memory capacity at approximately 7 ± 2 chunks.

quizQuestions.question: Better recall for items at the BEGINNING of a list is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The primacy effect occurs because early items get more rehearsal and are transferred to long-term memory.

quizQuestions.question: Anterograde amnesia is the inability to:
options: Recall memories from before an injury
Form new long-term memories after an injury
quizQuestions.answer: Form new long-term memories after an injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Anterograde amnesia affects the ability to create new long-term memories following brain damage.

quizQuestions.question: Elizabeth Loftus's research on eyewitness testimony showed that:
options: Memory is a perfect recording of past events
Post-event information can distort existing memories
Only emotional events are reliably remembered
quizQuestions.answer: Post-event information can distort existing memories
quizQuestions.explanation: Loftus demonstrated that misleading post-event information can alter or create false memories.

quizQuestions.question: Searching only for information that confirms existing beliefs is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias leads people to favor information consistent with their existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Howard Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences proposes that:
options: IQ is the most accurate measure of intelligence
Intelligence is a single general ability
quizQuestions.explanation: Gardner proposed at least 8 distinct intelligences (linguistic, logical-mathematical, musical, etc.).

quizQuestions.question: A mental shortcut that usually leads to correct answers but is not guaranteed is called a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Heuristics are cognitive shortcuts that allow for quick decisions with reasonable accuracy, though not guaranteed correctness.

quizQuestions.question: The Flynn Effect refers to:
options: Declining IQ scores in recent generations
Rising average IQ scores across populations over time
quizQuestions.answer: Rising average IQ scores across populations over time
quizQuestions.explanation: The Flynn Effect describes the consistent generational increase in average IQ scores observed across many nations.

quizQuestions.question: The Sapir-Whorf (linguistic relativity) hypothesis proposes that:
options: Language is universal and doesn't affect thought
The language we speak influences how we think
Thought develops completely independently of language
All languages share identical grammatical structures
quizQuestions.answer: The language we speak influences how we think
quizQuestions.explanation: Linguistic relativity suggests that the structure and vocabulary of one's language shape perception and cognition.

quizQuestions.question: Piaget's stage in which children first develop object permanence is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: Object permanence — knowing objects exist even when out of sight — develops during the sensorimotor stage (0–2 years).

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's stage of "Identity vs. Role Confusion" is most associated with:
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's fifth stage, Identity vs. Role Confusion, occurs during adolescence as individuals explore who they are.

quizQuestions.question: Mary Ainsworth's Strange Situation experiments were designed to reveal:
quizQuestions.explanation: Ainsworth identified secure, anxious-ambivalent, and avoidant attachment patterns using the Strange Situation procedure.

quizQuestions.question: Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) refers to:
options: Tasks a child can do alone
The gap between what a child can do alone versus with guidance
The period of peak cognitive development
quizQuestions.answer: The gap between what a child can do alone versus with guidance
quizQuestions.explanation: The ZPD identifies tasks a child cannot yet do independently but can accomplish with appropriate support (scaffolding).

quizQuestions.question: Kohlberg found that most adults operate primarily at which level of moral development?
quizQuestions.explanation: Most adults reason at the conventional level, making moral judgments based on social norms, laws, and maintaining relationships.

quizQuestions.question: According to Maslow's hierarchy, which need must be met before "belonging" needs are addressed?
quizQuestions.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy places safety needs (security, stability) below social belonging needs.

quizQuestions.question: The James-Lange theory of emotion proposes that emotions:
options: Are triggered by cognitive appraisal alone
quizQuestions.explanation: James-Lange: we feel afraid BECAUSE we run, not we run because we feel afraid — emotion follows bodily change.

quizQuestions.question: When someone loses interest in a hobby after being paid to do it, this is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The overjustification effect occurs when external rewards undermine intrinsic motivation.

quizQuestions.question: The Schachter-Singer two-factor theory of emotion emphasizes:
options: Hardwired emotional circuits in the brain
quizQuestions.explanation: Schachter-Singer: emotion requires both physiological arousal AND a cognitive label for that arousal.

quizQuestions.question: Intrinsic motivation refers to doing something:
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrinsic motivation comes from within — engaging in an activity because it is inherently enjoyable or meaningful.

quizQuestions.question: Freud's concept of the __________ mediates between the id's desires and the superego's moral demands.
quizQuestions.explanation: The ego operates on the reality principle, balancing the id's impulses with the superego's moral standards.

quizQuestions.question: According to Carl Rogers, a fully functioning person requires:
quizQuestions.explanation: Rogers believed healthy development requires acceptance and respect regardless of one's actions — unconditional positive regard.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the Big Five personality traits?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Big Five (OCEAN) are: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism.

quizQuestions.question: The Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI) is an example of a:
quizQuestions.explanation: The MMPI is a standardized self-report questionnaire used to assess personality traits and psychological disorders.

quizQuestions.question: The Rorschach inkblot test is classified as a:
quizQuestions.explanation: Projective tests present ambiguous stimuli for subjects to interpret, theoretically revealing unconscious thoughts.

quizQuestions.question: Attributing others' behavior to internal traits while ignoring situational factors is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The FAE describes the tendency to over-attribute others' behavior to character while under-weighting situational influences.

quizQuestions.question: Milgram's obedience experiments demonstrated that:
options: People rarely comply with authority figures
Ordinary people can follow orders to harm others
Conformity is more powerful than obedience
Social pressure has little effect on behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Ordinary people can follow orders to harm others
quizQuestions.explanation: Milgram found that 65% of participants delivered the maximum apparent shock under experimenter authority.

quizQuestions.question: Asch's conformity experiments showed that people gave incorrect answers primarily to:
options: Avoid standing out from the group
Test the limits of social norms
quizQuestions.answer: Avoid standing out from the group
quizQuestions.explanation: Most participants conformed to clearly wrong group answers to avoid social rejection or embarrassment.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive dissonance occurs when we:
options: Hold contradictory beliefs or act against our beliefs
quizQuestions.answer: Hold contradictory beliefs or act against our beliefs
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance is the psychological discomfort of holding inconsistent cognitions or acting contrary to one's beliefs.

quizQuestions.question: The bystander effect predicts individuals are __________ likely to help in an emergency when others are present.
quizQuestions.explanation: Diffusion of responsibility means each person feels less personally obligated to act when others are present.

quizQuestions.question: The primary classification system for mental disorders used in the U.S. is the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th edition (DSM-5) is published by the APA and is the standard classification tool in the U.S.

quizQuestions.question: Major depressive disorder is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Periods of elevated mood and grandiosity
quizQuestions.answer: Periods of elevated mood and grandiosity
quizQuestions.explanation: Elevated mood and grandiosity (mania) characterize bipolar disorder, not major depressive disorder.

quizQuestions.question: A person who checks whether the door is locked 40 times before leaving home most likely shows symptoms of:
quizQuestions.explanation: OCD is characterized by recurring obsessions (intrusive thoughts) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviors) that reduce anxiety.

quizQuestions.question: Schizophrenia is primarily characterized by:
options: Extreme mood swings between mania and depression
quizQuestions.explanation: Schizophrenia is a psychotic disorder featuring positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) and negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia).

quizQuestions.question: The diathesis-stress model of psychological disorders proposes that disorders result from:
options: A combination of genetic vulnerability and environmental stress
quizQuestions.answer: A combination of genetic vulnerability and environmental stress
quizQuestions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model: a biological predisposition (diathesis) combined with stressful life events triggers a disorder.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) focuses primarily on:
options: Changing maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors
quizQuestions.answer: Changing maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: CBT targets cognitive distortions (unhelpful thinking patterns) and uses behavioral techniques to create lasting change.

quizQuestions.question: Which type of therapy involves free association and dream analysis?
quizQuestions.explanation: Freudian psychoanalysis uses free association and dream interpretation to uncover unconscious conflicts.

quizQuestions.question: SSRIs are most commonly prescribed to treat:
quizQuestions.explanation: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are the first-line pharmacological treatment for depression and many anxiety disorders.

quizQuestions.question: Systematic desensitization treats phobias by:
options: Analyzing the unconscious roots of the fear
Pairing relaxation with gradual exposure to the feared stimulus
Removing the patient from anxiety-provoking situations
quizQuestions.answer: Pairing relaxation with gradual exposure to the feared stimulus
quizQuestions.explanation: Systematic desensitization uses a hierarchy of feared stimuli with relaxation training to extinguish the fear response.

quizQuestions.question: Research consistently shows that for most psychological disorders, the most effective treatment combines:
quizQuestions.explanation: Meta-analyses show that combined pharmacological and psychological treatment outperforms either approach alone for most disorders.
